text:
# ProShop MERN — Development History

> Internal document. Covers the full lifecycle of the project from initial commit (January 2023) through the current state (April 2026).

## 1. Project Timeline

### Phase 0 — Prototype (January–February 2023)

The project started as a learning exercise. The first working version had: a static product list seeded from JSON, a cart stored in `localStorage`, and no authentication. PayPal integration was mocked with a hardcoded `PAID` status flip. The team was two engineers working part-time.

Key early constraint: the team had prior experience with SQL (PostgreSQL) but chose MongoDB to experiment with document-based modeling for a product catalog with variable attributes. This decision is documented in ADR-001.

### Phase 1 — MVP (March–June 2023)

Authentication was added (JWT, bcrypt), the cart moved from localStorage to a proper Redux store, and orders became persistent in MongoDB. PayPal sandbox integration replaced the mock. An admin panel was scaffolded: product CRUD, order list, user management.

Deployment target: Heroku (free tier), with MongoDB Atlas on M0. The first user-facing bug that made it to production: the order total was calculated on the frontend and sent to the backend as-is. Fixed in v0.6 after a tester found they could manipulate the cart total in Redux DevTools and place orders at $0.01.

### Phase 2 — Stabilization (July–November 2023)

The app moved to Render.com after Heroku free tier ended. The review system (star rating + text comment) was added in v1.0. Redux was refactored from raw `redux` + hand-written reducers to **Redux Toolkit** (RTK) — the migration took three days and eliminated ~800 lines of boilerplate.

### Phase 3 — Growth Features (December 2023–June 2024)

v1.5 introduced image upload via Multer. v1.6 added pagination to the product list. v1.7 introduced the admin product edit screen and marked admin routes with a middleware guard. The Black Friday 2023 spike was the forcing function for connection pool tuning and proper load testing.

### Phase 4 — Hardening (July 2024–December 2024)

A security audit in July 2024 discovered that a `.env` file had been committed to the repository in February 2023 and never removed from git history (Incident i-003). JWT secrets, the MongoDB connection string, and PayPal credentials were all exposed. Full secret rotation and history rewrite followed.

v2.0 introduced rate limiting on auth endpoints and helmet.js for HTTP security headers.

### Phase 5 — Current (January 2025–April 2026)

The frontend is still running on CRA (officially unmaintained). RTK Query was evaluated as a replacement for the current Redux + `axios` data-fetching layer — POC showed 30–40% reduction in data-fetching code. Current deployment: Render.com (Node/Express), MongoDB Atlas M10 (dedicated), Cloudflare for DNS + DDoS protection.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN — Development History
metadata.summary: Full project timeline from January 2023 prototype through April 2026 covering all five development phases.

text:
## 2. Release Notes

### v0.1 — Initial Commit (2023-01-14)

**Added:** Project scaffold: Express backend, CRA frontend, concurrently dev runner. Static product data seeded from `backend/data/products.js`. MongoDB connection via Mongoose. Basic product listing and product detail pages.

**Known issues at release:** No authentication. Cart state lost on page refresh. No order flow.

### v0.3 — Cart + Basic Checkout (2023-02-08)

**Added:** Redux store with cart slice (localStorage persistence via `redux-persist`). Shipping address form, payment method selection. Place Order screen. PayPal mock: clicking "Pay" immediately flipped `isPaid: true`.

### v0.5 — Authentication (2023-03-15)

**Added:** User registration and login (bcrypt + JWT, 30-day expiry). Protected routes on frontend. Backend middleware: `protect` (auth check), first version of `admin` check.

**Breaking changes:** MongoDB schema for `Order` now requires `user` field.

### v0.6 — Order Total Recalculation Fix (2023-04-02)

**Fixed:** Critical: order totals now computed server-side in `orderController.createOrder`. Frontend-submitted totals are ignored. Tax rate hardcoded to 15%.

### v0.8 — PayPal SDK Integration (2023-04-28)

**Added:** Real PayPal JS SDK (sandbox) via `@paypal/react-paypal-js`. `PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID` served from backend env. Double-charge bug introduced in this release (not caught until v1.1 sandbox testing — see Incident i-001).

### v1.0 — Reviews + RTK Migration (2023-08-19)

**Added:** Product review system: star rating (1–5) + text comment, one review per user per product. Redux Toolkit migration complete: `createSlice`, `createAsyncThunk` across all feature slices.

**Fixed:** Admin middleware applied correctly to all mutation endpoints. JWT expiry now returns 401.

**Breaking changes:** Redux store shape changed. Clearing `localStorage` required for existing sessions.

### v1.2 — Pagination + Search (2023-10-05)

**Added:** Server-side pagination on `/api/products` (default 8 per page). Keyword search via MongoDB regex. `TAX_RATE` env variable.

**Performance:** Home screen initial load: 1.8s → 420ms (p50) after pagination.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN — Development History
metadata.summary: Release notes for v0.1 through v1.2 covering initial scaffold, auth, order total fix, PayPal SDK, and RTK migration.

text:
## 2. Release Notes (continued)

### v1.5 — Image Upload + Admin Edit (2024-01-22)

**Added:** Multer-based image upload to local `uploads/` directory. Admin product edit screen. Admin user edit (name, email, isAdmin toggle). Admin order list with delivery status update.

**Known limitation:** Image uploads not shared across instances. Single-instance constraint documented.

### v1.7 — Route Security Patch (2024-03-14)

**Fixed (Critical):** `PUT /api/products/:id` and `DELETE /api/products/:id` now require both `protect` and `admin` middleware. Previously only `protect` was applied — any authenticated user could modify or delete products. `PUT /api/users/:id` similarly hardened.

**Added:** Integration test suite (Jest + Supertest) covering auth flows and product CRUD — 34 tests, 87% coverage on controllers.

### v2.0 — Security Hardening Release (2024-08-12)

**Added:** `helmet.js` (HTTP security headers). `express-rate-limit` on `/api/users/login` and `/api/users/register`. `cors` configuration locked to allowed origins. `git-secrets` pre-commit hook. GitHub Actions: credential scan + dependency audit on every push.

**Fixed:** `.env` removed from git history (see Incident i-003). All secrets rotated.

### v2.3 — PayPal Production + Rate Limiting Tuning (2024-10-07)

**Added:** PayPal production credentials (sandbox retired). Separate rate limit config for admin endpoints (60 req/15 min). Morgan request logging to file (rotated daily, 14-day retention). PayPal webhook idempotency check closed after 60-day sandbox verification.

### v2.5 — Connection Pool + Atlas Upgrade (2025-02-18)

**Added:** Mongoose connection pool size set to `maxPoolSize: 50` (was default 5). MongoDB Atlas upgraded from M2 to M10 (dedicated cluster). `mongodbAtlasAlerts` configured: CPU >70%, connections >80%. Health check endpoint `/api/health` returning DB ping latency.

### v2.7 — Current Stable (2025-09-03)

**Added:** Cloudflare integration (DNS, CDN, DDoS protection). Product `countInStock` enforced at order creation. `express-validator` on all mutation endpoints. Structured JSON logging.

**Fixed:** Review endpoint allowed unauthenticated POST if token was malformed. Order pagination on admin order list.

**Deprecations:** `redux-persist` will be removed in v3.0.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN — Development History
metadata.summary: Release notes for v1.5 through v2.7 covering image upload, security patches, hardening, PayPal production, and Atlas upgrade.

text:
## 3. Major Decisions

### Decision 1: MongoDB over PostgreSQL

Made in January 2023. The product catalog has variable attributes (electronics have wattage/voltage, clothing has size/material) that would require EAV patterns or JSONB columns in PostgreSQL. MongoDB's flexible document model handles this naturally.

In retrospect: for the core e-commerce entities, a relational model would have been equally fine and would have given stronger consistency guarantees. The schema flexibility benefit was mostly theoretical — in practice, the product schema stabilized within two months.

### Decision 2: Redux Toolkit migration (v1.0)

The original Redux setup had grown to ~1,200 lines of boilerplate for six slices. RTK's `createSlice` and `createAsyncThunk` cut this to ~400 lines with equivalent functionality. A second migration is scoped: moving from RTK + axios to RTK Query — the POC showed ~60% reduction in custom async thunk code.

### Decision 3: Rejecting SSR / Next.js (late 2024)

Migrating to Next.js was evaluated to improve SEO. Conclusion: the migration cost (rewiring the Express API layer, adapting Redux to work with server components, Vercel deployment model change) exceeded the projected SEO benefit given current traffic volume. If started today, Next.js or Remix would be the default choice.

### Decision 4: Staying on Bootstrap 4

Bootstrap 4 was chosen in January 2023 because it was the default in the project template. By 2024, Tailwind CSS had become the clear community preference. The migration was scoped at ~5 engineer-days. Given other priorities, it has been deferred twice.

### Decision 5: JWT over session cookies

Chosen for stateless architecture — the backend has no session store, making horizontal scaling simpler. The main operational cost: no server-side session revocation (a compromised token remains valid until expiry). Mitigated by short-ish expiry (30 days) and secret rotation capability.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN — Development History
metadata.summary: Five major architectural decisions: MongoDB choice, RTK migration, rejecting Next.js, Bootstrap 4 stay, and JWT vs sessions.

text:
## 4. Lessons Learned

### What worked well

**Redux Toolkit from the start would have saved a week.** The original hand-rolled Redux setup was a learning exercise, but the migration effort in v1.0 was real cost.

**MongoDB Atlas monitoring is underrated.** After the i-002 incident, enabling Atlas performance advisor and configuring connection alerts caught two other near-misses. The built-in query profiler identified a missing index on `orders.user` that caused a 2.1s query on the admin order list.

**`express-async-handler` is a legitimate solution.** Early reviews suggested it was "hacky." In practice it's been stable for three years and keeps the controller code clean.

**Integration tests on the controller layer paid off.** The 34 tests added in v1.7 caught three regressions during later refactors, including a middleware ordering bug that would have re-opened the admin route vulnerability.

### What we'd do differently

**Server-side recalculation of order totals should have been in v0.1, not v0.6.** Trusting the client for any financial calculation is never acceptable.

**The `.env` commit was preventable.** `git-secrets` or a `.gitignore` template that includes `.env` by default would have caught it. It stayed in git history for 17 months before discovery.

**Image uploads should have gone to S3 from the start.** The `uploads/` local directory approach created a silent coupling between deployment topology and feature functionality.

**PayPal over Stripe was the wrong call.** PayPal's sandbox environment is notoriously unreliable — the double-charge incident was caused by non-standard webhook behavior that only occurs in sandbox. Stripe's test mode is a much more faithful replica of production behavior.

**CRA lock-in should have been addressed sooner.** Starting with Vite would have avoided the current situation where upgrading the build tool requires untangling Bootstrap 4 and legacy Babel config simultaneously.

**No load testing until after the first incident.** A basic `k6` smoke test would have revealed the connection pool ceiling in 20 minutes.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN — Development History
metadata.summary: Retrospective on what worked well and what the team would do differently across auth, payments, uploads, and tooling.

text:
# Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN

> This document is the authoritative reference for the `features.json` file and the MCP server that manages it.

## 1. Introduction

### What Are Feature Flags?

A **feature flag** (also called a feature toggle, feature switch, or feature gate) is a software engineering technique that lets you turn functionality on or off without deploying new code. Instead of shipping a feature to all users at once, you ship the code behind a conditional check, then control visibility through a configuration file — in this project, `features.json`.

Feature flags solve a core tension in software delivery: teams want to ship code frequently (to avoid merge conflicts and integration hell), but they also want to protect end users from half-finished or risky changes. The flag is the mechanism that decouples **code deployment** from **feature release**.

### Why Do Feature Flags Matter in Production Systems?

**Gradual rollout (Canary Deployment).** You enable a redesigned checkout flow for 5% of users, monitor error rates and conversion metrics, then expand to 25%, 50%, and finally 100% if no regressions appear. If something breaks, you turn the flag off — no rollback deploy required.

**A/B Testing.** You split traffic 50/50 with `status: "Testing"` and `traffic_percentage: 50`, instrument your analytics events, run for two weeks, and pick the winner.

**Kill Switch.** An external payment provider has a service outage. You disable `stripe_alternative` in seconds, routing all traffic back to PayPal. No code changes, no redeployment.

**Beta and Internal Testing.** Set `status: "Testing"`, `traffic_percentage: 100`, and `targeted_segments: ["admin", "internal"]`.

### Feature Flags in This Project

The `features.json` file defines 25 feature flags covering the full surface area of the application. Your MCP server reads this file and exposes three tools that allow an AI assistant to query and modify flags in real time.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Introduces feature flags concept, explains use cases (canary, A/B testing, kill switch), and describes the 25-flag features.json file.

text:
## 2. The `features.json` Format

### Required Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | string | Human-readable display name of the feature. |
| `description` | string | Detailed explanation of what the feature does and why it exists. |
| `status` | string | One of: `"Disabled"`, `"Testing"`, `"Enabled"`. |
| `traffic_percentage` | number | Integer 0–100. Percentage of eligible requests that will have this feature active. |
| `last_modified` | string | ISO 8601 date (`YYYY-MM-DD`) of the most recent change. MCP server must update this field on every write. |

### Optional Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `targeted_segments` | array of strings | User segments: `"beta_users"`, `"admin"`, `"guests"`, `"authenticated"`, `"mobile"`, `"internal"`, `"safari_users"`, `"all"`. |
| `rollout_strategy` | string | One of: `"canary"`, `"ab_test"`, `"full_release"`. |
| `dependencies` | array of strings | Feature IDs that must be `"Enabled"` before this flag can be meaningfully activated. MCP server should warn (but not block) when dependencies are `"Disabled"`. |

### Status Semantics

**`"Disabled"`** — The feature is completely off. `traffic_percentage` must be `0`. Safe default for any new flag.

**`"Testing"`** — The feature is active for a controlled subset of traffic defined by `traffic_percentage` and optionally `targeted_segments`. `traffic_percentage` can be 1 to 99.

**`"Enabled"`** — The feature is fully live for all eligible traffic. `traffic_percentage` is `100`. Once a flag reaches this state, schedule it for cleanup within the next release cycle.

### Example Feature Object

```json
{
  "search_v2": {
    "name": "New Search Algorithm",
    "description": "Replaces legacy regex-based keyword matching with a hybrid BM25 + TF-IDF ranking pipeline.",
    "status": "Testing",
    "traffic_percentage": 15,
    "last_modified": "2026-03-10",
    "targeted_segments": ["beta_users", "internal"],
    "rollout_strategy": "canary"
  }
}
```

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Defines the features.json schema with required/optional fields and semantics of Disabled, Testing, and Enabled status values.

text:
## 3. MCP Server Tool Contract

### Tool 1: `get_feature_info`

**Purpose:** Retrieve the complete current state of a single feature flag.

**Signature:** `get_feature_info(feature_id: string) -> FeatureObject | ErrorObject`

**Return value on success:** Returns the full feature object with all fields, plus the `feature_id` echoed back.

**Error conditions:**

| Error code | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `FEATURE_NOT_FOUND` | `feature_id` does not exist as a key in `features.json`. |
| `FILE_READ_ERROR` | `features.json` cannot be read (permissions, missing file). |
| `JSON_PARSE_ERROR` | `features.json` exists but contains invalid JSON. |

**Example call:**

> "What is the current status of the dark mode feature?"

The assistant calls: `{ "tool": "get_feature_info", "arguments": { "feature_id": "dark_mode" } }`

The server returns the full object. Assistant answers: "Dark mode is currently in Testing at 20% traffic, targeting all users."

### Tool 2: `set_feature_state`

**Purpose:** Change the `status` of a feature flag. Automatically adjusts `traffic_percentage` to the canonical value and updates `last_modified`.

**Signature:** `set_feature_state(feature_id: string, state: "Disabled" | "Testing" | "Enabled") -> FeatureObject | ErrorObject`

**Side effects:**
- Transitioning to `"Disabled"`: sets `traffic_percentage` to `0`.
- Transitioning to `"Enabled"`: sets `traffic_percentage` to `100`.
- Transitioning to `"Testing"`: leaves `traffic_percentage` unchanged if 1–99; otherwise sets it to `10`.
- Always updates `last_modified` to today's date.
- If the feature has `dependencies` and new state is `"Testing"` or `"Enabled"`, check each dependency — if any dependency is not `"Enabled"`, include a `warnings` array.

**Error conditions:** `FEATURE_NOT_FOUND`, `INVALID_STATE`, `FILE_READ_ERROR`, `FILE_WRITE_ERROR`.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: MCP server tool contracts for get_feature_info and set_feature_state including side effects and error conditions.

text:
### Tool 3: `adjust_traffic_rollout`

**Purpose:** Change the `traffic_percentage` of a feature currently in `"Testing"` state. Does not change the `status`. Updates `last_modified`.

**Signature:** `adjust_traffic_rollout(feature_id: string, percentage: number) -> FeatureObject | ErrorObject`

**Validation rules:**
- `percentage` must be an integer (no decimals).
- `percentage` must be in the range 0–100.
- The feature's `status` must be `"Testing"`. If the feature is `"Disabled"` or `"Enabled"`, return an error.

**Side effects:**
- Sets `traffic_percentage` to the provided value.
- Updates `last_modified` to today's date.
- If `percentage` is `0`, include a `hint` suggesting the caller use `set_feature_state` with `"Disabled"` instead.
- If `percentage` is `100`, include a `hint` suggesting promotion to `"Enabled"` via `set_feature_state`.

**Error conditions:**

| Error code | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `FEATURE_NOT_FOUND` | `feature_id` does not exist. |
| `WRONG_STATUS_FOR_ROLLOUT` | Feature `status` is not `"Testing"`. |
| `INVALID_PERCENTAGE` | `percentage` is not an integer, or is outside 0–100. |
| `FILE_READ_ERROR` | Cannot read `features.json`. |
| `FILE_WRITE_ERROR` | Cannot write the updated `features.json`. |

**Example call:**

> "Expand the dark mode test from 20% to 50%."

The assistant calls: `{ "tool": "adjust_traffic_rollout", "arguments": { "feature_id": "dark_mode", "percentage": 50 } }`

The server writes the update. Response includes `traffic_percentage: 50` and updated `last_modified`.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: MCP tool contract for adjust_traffic_rollout including validation rules, error conditions, and hint behavior at 0% and 100%.

text:
## 4. Feature Flag Catalog — Search & Discovery, Cart

### Search & Discovery

#### `search_v2` — New Search Algorithm
**Default state:** Testing (15%)

Replaces `getProducts` MongoDB `$regex` with a hybrid BM25 + TF-IDF ranking pipeline that indexes `name`, `brand`, `category`, and `description`. Adds fuzzy tolerance for single-character misspellings.

**System impact:** `backend/controllers/productController.js`, MongoDB text index on Product collection. No frontend changes.
**Typical rollout:** 5% internal → 25% → 50% A/B comparison of zero-result rate → 100%.
**Dependencies:** None. Prerequisite for `semantic_search`.

#### `semantic_search` — Semantic Vector Search
**Default state:** Disabled

Extends `search_v2` with embedding-based retrieval. Product embeddings pre-computed and stored; at query time, user's search string is embedded and matched by cosine similarity.

**System impact:** New embedding pipeline (background job), vector index on Product collection, extended productController search endpoint. High infrastructure cost.
**Dependencies:** `search_v2` must be `"Enabled"` first.

#### `search_autosuggest` — Search Autocomplete
**Default state:** Testing (25%)

As the user types in `SearchBox`, a dropdown shows matching product names and popular query strings. Calls debounced to a lightweight `/api/products/suggest` endpoint.

**System impact:** New backend route `/api/products/suggest`, minor extension to `SearchBox` component.

### Cart

#### `cart_redesign` — Redesigned Cart UI
**Default state:** Testing (10%)

Sticky sidebar summary, inline quantity spinners, animated item removal, and a coupon code input field placeholder. Internal testing shows a 40% reduction in clicks to reach checkout.
**System impact:** React component (`CartScreen.js`) only. No backend changes.
**Dependencies:** `save_for_later` depends on this flag.

#### `save_for_later` — Save Items for Later
**Default state:** Disabled

Adds a "Save for Later" button. Saved items persisted to the authenticated user's profile in MongoDB (requires `savedItems` array field added to `userModel`).
**System impact:** `userModel.js` schema extension, new API route `POST /api/users/saved-items`.
**Dependencies:** `cart_redesign`.

#### `guest_cart_persistence` — Guest Cart Persistence
**Default state:** Enabled (100%)

Controls restoring the cart on the next browser session and displaying a "Welcome back — your cart has been saved" banner. Fully client-side.
**Dependencies:** None.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Feature catalog entries for search (search_v2, semantic_search, autosuggest) and cart (redesign, save_for_later, guest persistence) flags.

text:
## 4. Feature Flag Catalog — Checkout, Payments

### Checkout

#### `express_checkout` — Express One-Click Checkout
**Default state:** Disabled

Authenticated users who have previously completed an order see a "Buy Now" button on `ProductScreen` and `CartScreen`. Bypasses the four-step checkout flow.
**System impact:** New `POST /api/orders/express` endpoint.
**Dependencies:** `guest_cart_persistence`.

#### `multi_step_checkout_v2` — Redesigned Multi-Step Checkout
**Default state:** Testing (20%)

Replaces hard redirects between `ShippingScreen → PaymentScreen → PlaceOrderScreen` with an animated stepper on a single URL. Users can navigate backward without losing entered data. Includes real-time address validation.
**System impact:** New `CheckoutWizard` React component replaces three existing screen components. Router configuration change in `App.js`. No backend changes.
**Typical rollout:** A/B test at 50/50. Target: 15% improvement in checkout completion rate.
**Dependencies:** None.

#### `gift_message` — Gift Message at Checkout
**Default state:** Disabled

Optional textarea on `PlaceOrderScreen` (max 280 characters). Stored in Order document as `giftMessage: String`.
**System impact:** `orderModel.js` (new optional field), `PlaceOrderScreen.js`, `orderController.addOrderItems`.
**Dependencies:** None.

### Payments

#### `paypal_express_buttons` — PayPal Express Checkout Buttons
**Default state:** Enabled (100%)

PayPal Smart Payment Buttons appear on `CartScreen` and `ProductScreen` (above the fold). Serves as a kill switch — if PayPal has a service incident, set to Disabled immediately.
**Dependencies:** None.

#### `apple_pay` — Apple Pay
**Default state:** Disabled

Apple Pay button on `PaymentScreen` for Safari users on iOS and macOS. Merchant domain verification must be completed before enabling. Payment processor: Stripe.
**Dependencies:** `stripe_alternative` must be active.

#### `stripe_alternative` — Stripe as Alternative Payment Processor
**Default state:** Testing (5%)

Enables the Stripe payment path: user selects Stripe, enters card details in Stripe Elements form. Acts as a redundant payment path when PayPal is unavailable.
**System impact:** `PaymentScreen.js` (uncomment + wrap in flag check), new backend route `POST /api/orders/stripe-pay`, Stripe webhook handler.
**Dependencies:** None.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Feature catalog entries for checkout flags (express checkout, multi-step v2, gift message) and payment flags (PayPal, Apple Pay, Stripe).

text:
## 4. Feature Flag Catalog — Catalog, Admin, Reviews, Performance, UX

### Catalog & Discovery

#### `product_recommendations` — Product Recommendations Engine
**Default state:** Testing (30%)

"Customers also bought" section on `ProductScreen`. Initial version uses a co-purchase matrix from existing orders. Falls back to `getTopProducts` when insufficient co-purchase data.
**System impact:** New `GET /api/products/:id/recommendations`, new `RecommendationRow` component.

#### `recently_viewed` — Recently Viewed Products
**Default state:** Enabled (100%)

Last 6 product detail pages tracked in `localStorage`. Shown at the bottom of `HomeScreen` and `ProductScreen`. Fully client-side.

#### `infinite_scroll` — Infinite Scroll on Product Listing
**Default state:** Disabled

Replaces `Paginate` component with `IntersectionObserver`-based infinite loader. Target mobile users first.

### Admin

#### `admin_dashboard_v2` — Redesigned Admin Dashboard
**Default state:** Disabled

Unified admin dashboard featuring revenue KPI cards, low-stock alert widget, orders-per-day bar chart, and collapsible sidebar. Enable for `admin` segment only.
**Dependencies:** `admin_bulk_actions` and `admin_advanced_filters` build on top of this UI.

#### `admin_bulk_actions` — Bulk Product Actions
**Default state:** Disabled

Checkboxes on admin product list. Actions: bulk delete, apply percentage discount, reassign category. Uses `PATCH /api/products/bulk`.
**Dependencies:** `admin_dashboard_v2`.

#### `admin_advanced_filters` — Advanced Filters in Admin
**Default state:** Testing (100%, admin segment)

Multi-criteria filter panels for order and product list screens: order status, date range, payment method, price range, stock level.

### Reviews

#### `reviews_moderation` — Admin Review Moderation Queue
**Default state:** Disabled

New reviews saved with `status: "pending"` instead of going live immediately. Admins see a moderation queue.
**System impact:** `reviewSchema` extension (add `status` field), new admin endpoint `PATCH /api/products/:id/reviews/:reviewId/moderate`.

#### `photo_reviews` — Photo Attachments in Reviews
**Default state:** Disabled

Customers can attach up to 3 photos when writing a review. Uploaded to existing `/api/upload` endpoint. Stored as `images: [String]` in `reviewSchema`.
**Dependencies:** `reviews_moderation` recommended.

#### `verified_purchase_badge` — Verified Purchase Badge
**Default state:** Enabled (100%)

Reviews by users with a completed, delivered order containing the reviewed product display a "Verified Purchase" badge.

### Performance & UX

#### `image_lazy_loading` — Lazy Loading for Product Images
**Default state:** Enabled (100%)

Native `loading="lazy"` applied to all product images. Reduces initial page load weight by 60–70% on catalog pages.

#### `code_splitting_optimisation` — Route-Level Code Splitting
**Default state:** Testing (50%)

All screen-level React components wrapped in `React.lazy()`. Reduces initial JS bundle from ~280 KB to ~90 KB for anonymous users.

#### `dark_mode` — Dark Mode Theme
**Default state:** Testing (20%)

Sun/moon toggle in `Header` switches between light and dark CSS custom property themes. Preference persisted to `localStorage`. Respects OS-level `prefers-color-scheme` as initial default.

#### `guest_checkout` — Guest Checkout Without Registration
**Default state:** Disabled

Unauthenticated users can complete a purchase without creating an account. Estimated 15-25% recovery of checkout-abandonment from registration friction.
**System impact:** New backend middleware for guest token issuance, `orderModel` extension for guest orders.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Feature catalog for catalog discovery, admin, reviews, performance, and UX flags covering remaining 15 of 25 features.

text:
## 5. Rollout Strategies

### Canary Deployment

A canary deployment introduces a change to a small percentage of production traffic before full rollout.

**Standard canary ladder for this project:**

```
5% → 25% → 50% → 100% → promote to Enabled
```

Each step requires at minimum 48 hours of observation. Metrics to watch: error rate (HTTP 5xx on affected endpoints), p95 latency, conversion rate on the affected flow.

**MCP tool sequence for a canary rollout:**

```
1. set_feature_state("search_v2", "Testing")       → traffic_percentage becomes 10
2. adjust_traffic_rollout("search_v2", 25)          → after 48h with no regressions
3. adjust_traffic_rollout("search_v2", 50)          → after another 48h
4. adjust_traffic_rollout("search_v2", 100)         → soak for 24h
5. set_feature_state("search_v2", "Enabled")        → lock in the change
```

### A/B Testing

Set `status: "Testing"`, `traffic_percentage: 50`, `rollout_strategy: "ab_test"`. Define your primary metric before starting. Run for a minimum of two weeks to avoid day-of-week bias.

After the test: Winner → `set_feature_state` to `"Enabled"`. Loser → `set_feature_state` to `"Disabled"`, then schedule for code cleanup.

### Kill Switch

The kill switch pattern means keeping a fully-deployed feature under a flag that remains Enabled in normal operation but can be disabled instantly.

`paypal_express_buttons`, `recently_viewed`, `verified_purchase_badge`, and `image_lazy_loading` are all currently Enabled and serve as kill switches.

**Kill switch activation:**
```
set_feature_state("paypal_express_buttons", "Disabled")
```

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Canary deployment ladder with MCP tool sequence, A/B testing setup and teardown, and kill switch activation pattern.

text:
## 6. Best Practices

### Flag Lifecycle and Cleanup Policy

**The lifecycle:**
1. **Create** the flag in `features.json` with `status: "Disabled"`.
2. **Test** (canary or A/B).
3. **Enable** (promote to 100%).
4. **Clean up** — remove the flag from `features.json` and remove the conditional in code within 30 days of reaching `"Enabled"`. Create a backlog ticket at promotion time.

**Signals that a flag is overdue for cleanup:**
- `status` has been `"Enabled"` for more than 30 days.
- The flag has no `targeted_segments`.
- No active rollout or A/B test is referencing it.

### Avoiding Flag Dependency Chains

- Maximum dependency depth: 2 levels.
- Never create a circular dependency.
- When a flag graduates to `"Enabled"`, its dependents should be updated to remove the dependency reference.

### Naming Conventions

- Feature IDs are `snake_case`, lowercase, no hyphens.
- Use a noun-verb or noun-adjective structure: `cart_redesign`, `search_v2`, `photo_reviews`.
- Version suffixes (`_v2`, `_v3`) signal replacement of an existing feature.
- Avoid generic names like `new_feature` or `experiment_1`.

### Testing Flags in Development

When writing unit tests for code guarded by a flag, always write tests for both the `Enabled` and `Disabled` paths.

### Security Considerations

`features.json` is a configuration file that controls production behavior. The MCP server that writes it should require authentication — do not expose the write tools publicly. Treat `targeted_segments` values as non-secret labels.

metadata.title: Feature Flags Specification — ProShop MERN
metadata.summary: Feature flag best practices covering lifecycle cleanup policy, dependency chain rules, naming conventions, and security considerations.

text:
# Анализ feature flags ProShop MERN — навигация по M3 → M7

> Этот документ — спутник к `features.json` и `feature-flags-spec.md`. Здесь нет нового контракта MCP; здесь — карта 25 фичей с трудозатратами, ролью в курсе и кандидатами на прототипирование в M4.

## TL;DR

- **`features.json` оставляем как есть — все 25 фичей.** Разнообразие статусов, стратегий, зависимостей и сегментов нужно, чтобы MCP-сервер из Части 1 M3 валидировался на реальных кейсах.
- **Якорная фича курса (M3 → M7): `multi_step_checkout_v2`.** Код базы уже есть (`frontend/src/components/CheckoutSteps.js`), стратегия `ab_test`, трафик 20% — идеальная сцена под весь сквозной сценарий.
- **На M4 Figma-прототип** — топ-4 кандидата: `multi_step_checkout_v2`, `cart_redesign`, `admin_dashboard_v2`, `dark_mode`.
- **Аномалии в данных** (4 шт.) и **рассинхроны «флаг включён, кода нет»** (4 шт.) — это реалистичные учебные артефакты.

## 1. Что считать «обязательной» фичей для М3 практика 1

**`multi_step_checkout_v2`.** Это та фича, которую студенты обязаны сохранить в `features.json` и на которой будет демонстрационный прогон MCP-сервера.

| Критерий | `multi_step_checkout_v2` | Альтернативы |
|---|---|---|
| Код в репо уже есть | ✅ `CheckoutSteps.js` 4-шаговый чекаут готов | `dark_mode` — нет; `cart_redesign` — частично |
| Видимый UX-импакт | ✅ 4 экрана: shipping → payment → review → place | `recently_viewed` — узкая полка |
| Естественный A/B (стратегия `ab_test`) | ✅ 50/50 split на конверсию | `search_v2` — канарейка, не A/B |
| Конверсионная метрика для Sentry M6/M7 | ✅ checkout error rate, drop-off per step | `image_lazy_loading` — невидимая |
| Сценарий «n8n агент крутит % трафика» в M5 | ✅ 0 → 20 → 50 → 100 пошагово | `paypal_express_buttons` — уже 100% |
| Богатый Figma-материал для M4 | ✅ stepper, прогресс-индикатор, инлайн-валидация | большинство мелких фичей не дают |

metadata.title: Анализ feature flags ProShop MERN — навигация по M3 → M7

metadata.summary: Определяет multi_step_checkout_v2 как якорную фичу курса M3–M7 и обосновывает выбор по шести критериям.

text:
## 2. Глоссарий стратегий раскатки

В `rollout_strategy` три значения. Они НЕ синонимы статуса — это про *как* выкатывают, а не про *включено ли*.

| `rollout_strategy` | По-русски | Что значит | Когда выбирают |
|---|---|---|---|
| `canary` | Канареечная раскатка | Включают на маленькой группе (5–25%), смотрят метрики, расширяют по шагам: 5 → 25 → 50 → 100. Если что-то ломается — мгновенный откат флипом флага, без редеплоя. | Рискованные изменения: новая логика поиска, новый платёжный провайдер, перформанс-фичи. |
| `ab_test` | A/B-тест | Трафик делится на две группы (часто 50/50), обе группы видят разные версии, сравниваются метрики (конверсия, AOV, time-on-page). Победитель уезжает в `Enabled`, проигравший — в `Disabled`. | Гипотезы про UX: одностраничная корзина vs двухколоночная, степпер vs линейный чекаут, тёмная тема. |
| `full_release` | Полный релиз | Бинарный выключатель: либо включено для всех (100%), либо выключено. Без промежуточных %. | Бэйджи, мелкие UI-улучшения, lazy-loading, опциональные поля типа «подарочное сообщение». |

**Важно:** `traffic_percentage` имеет разный смысл при разных стратегиях:
- `canary` — % реальных юзеров, видящих фичу.
- `ab_test` — % юзеров в variant B (остальные в A).
- `full_release` — формально либо 0, либо 100; промежуточные значения — аномалия.

metadata.title: Анализ feature flags ProShop MERN — навигация по M3 → M7
metadata.summary: Объясняет три стратегии раскатки (canary, ab_test, full_release) и разный смысл traffic_percentage для каждой из них.

metadata.summary: Полная таблица всех 25 feature flags с кодом в репо, сложностью 1–10, приоритетом и оценкой для Figma M4.

text: ## 4. Анализ аномалий в данных В `feature-flags-spec.md` бизнес-правила сформулированы строго: `Disabled → traffic = 0`, `traffic > 0 → status ≠ Disabled`. Но в `features.json` есть несколько мест, где правила нарушены. Это не баги — это реалистичные учебные артефакты. ### Аномалии стратегий | Фича | Что не так | Как трактовать | |---|---|---| | `admin_advanced_filters` | `status=Testing` + `traffic=100%` + `strategy=full_release` | Если 100% и `full_release` — должен быть `Enabled`. Похоже, флаг забыли промоутить. | | `gift_message` | `strategy=full_release` + `status=Disabled` | Стратегия «полный релиз», но фича выключена. Возможно, готовили к запуску и решили отложить. | | `admin_bulk_actions` | `strategy=full_release` + `status=Disabled` | То же. | | `reviews_moderation` | `strategy=full_release` + `status=Disabled` | То же. | ### Рассинхроны «флаг включён, кода нет» Эти фичи отмечены `Enabled / 100%` в `features.json`, но в кодовой базе `proshop_mern` соответствующая логика отсутствует. | Фича | Состояние флага | Состояние кода | |---|---|---| | `recently_viewed` | Enabled / 100% | Нет ни reducer'а, ни UI | | `verified_purchase_badge` | Enabled / 100% | Нет проверки delivered-order, нет бейджа | | `image_lazy_loading` | Enabled / 100% | В `Product.js` обычный `` без `loading="lazy"` | | `admin_advanced_filters` | Testing / 100% | Фильтров в `OrderListScreen` / `ProductListScreen` нет | **Учебный материал на M5–M6:** «как агент должен ловить такой дрейф». Темы: автоматическая проверка наличия флаговых веток в коде (статанализ или sentry breadcrumbs), регулярная синхронизация конфига с реальностью.

metadata.title: Анализ feature flags ProShop MERN — навигация по M3 → M7
metadata.summary: Описывает 4 аномалии стратегий и 4 рассинхрона «флаг включён, кода нет» как учебные артефакты для M5–M6.

text:
## 5. M4 Figma-прототипирование: кандидаты

### ⭐⭐⭐ Топ-уровень — сильно рекомендую для M4 домашки

| Фича | Чем хороша для Figma |
|---|---|
| `multi_step_checkout_v2` | Степпер с прогресс-индикатором, анимированные панели, инлайн-валидация адреса. |
| `cart_redesign` | Однополосный layout, sticky-сайдбар, анимация удаления, инлайн-редактирование количества. Гипотеза «-40% кликов до checkout». |
| `admin_dashboard_v2` | KPI-карточки, графики, фильтры, единый сайдбар. Целая B2B-плоскость. |
| `dark_mode` | Темизация всего приложения. Учит работе с CSS variables, токенами цвета, дизайн-системой. |

### ⭐⭐ Хорошие кандидаты

`search_autosuggest`, `product_recommendations`, `save_for_later`, `photo_reviews`, `express_checkout`, `reviews_moderation`.

### ❌ Не для Figma — невидимые / чисто бэкенд

`search_v2`, `semantic_search`, `paypal_express_buttons`, `apple_pay`, `image_lazy_loading`, `code_splitting_optimisation`, `guest_cart_persistence`.

## 6. Резюме рекомендаций

1. **`features.json` оставить как есть, все 25 фичей.**
2. **На М3 практика 1 фокусируйтесь на `multi_step_checkout_v2`.** Замените `search_v2` в тестовом промпте Части 1 на неё.
3. **На M4 (Figma) дайте студентам выбор из 4 топов.** `multi_step_checkout_v2` — обязательный демонстрационный экран; студенты выбирают из `cart_redesign`, `admin_dashboard_v2`, `dark_mode`.
4. **На M5 (n8n агент) — сценарий вокруг `multi_step_checkout_v2`:** 0% → 20% → 50% → 100% по триггерам Sentry-метрик.
5. **На M6/M7 (Sentry / observability) — флаги-«рассинхроны».** Показать 4 кейса как реальную production-проблему.
6. **Skip-фичи** оставить в `features.json` как примеры внешних зависимостей, но в живом проекте не реализовывать.

metadata.title: Анализ feature flags ProShop MERN — навигация по M3 → M7
metadata.summary: Рейтинг фичей для Figma-прототипирования в M4 и итоговые рекомендации по использованию флагов в курсе M3–M7.

text:
## Domain Terms (E-Commerce Concepts)

### Customer
A user registered in the system who can browse products, add items to cart, and place orders. Customers have authentication credentials (email/password) and order history. Related: User, Account.

**In proshop:** Stored in MongoDB `users` collection with `isAdmin: false`. Example: john@example.com.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Customer as a registered user with credentials and order history, stored in MongoDB users collection.

text:
### Product
A physical or digital good offered for sale, with a unique price, description, inventory count, and reviews. Products are created and managed by admin users. A product always has a category, brand, and in-stock count.

**In proshop:** `Product` model with fields: `name`, `price`, `countInStock`, `category`, `brand`, `description`, `reviews[]`, `rating`, `numReviews`. Accessible via `/api/products` endpoint.

**Example:**
```json
{
  "_id": "60d5ec49c1234567890abcde",
  "name": "iPhone 15 Pro",
  "price": 999.99,
  "countInStock": 15,
  "category": "Electronics",
  "brand": "Apple",
  "rating": 4.8,
  "numReviews": 42
}
```

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Product with its schema fields including price, countInStock, category, brand, reviews, and the /api/products endpoint.

text:
### Order
A confirmed purchase by a customer containing one or more order items, shipping address, payment method, and totals (tax, shipping, total). Orders track payment status (`isPaid`, `paidAt`) and delivery status (`isDelivered`, `deliveredAt`).

**In proshop:** `Order` model with `user` reference, `orderItems[]`, `shippingAddress`, `paymentMethod`, `totalPrice`, `isPaid`, `isDelivered`. Once an order is placed, inventory is decremented (if implemented).

**Lifecycle:** Created → Awaiting payment → Paid → Shipped → Delivered.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Order with its lifecycle from creation to delivery and key fields for payment and delivery tracking.

text:
### Order Item
A single line-item in an order, representing one product with a quantity, price at time of purchase, and product reference. Order items are immutable snapshots—if product price changes later, the order item keeps the original price.

**In proshop:** Embedded in `Order.orderItems[]` with fields: `product` (ObjectId ref), `name`, `qty`, `price`, `image`.

**Example:**
```json
{
  "product": "60d5ec49c1234567890abcde",
  "name": "iPhone 15 Pro",
  "qty": 2,
  "price": 999.99,
  "image": "/images/iphone15.jpg"
}
```

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Order Item as an immutable snapshot embedded in Order.orderItems with price captured at purchase time.

text:
### Cart
Temporary collection of products selected by a customer but not yet ordered. Carts exist client-side (Redux state) and are not persisted to database in proshop v1. When checkout completes, cart items become order items.

**In proshop:** Managed in Redux (`frontend/src/slices/cartSlice.js`). Has `cartItems[]`, `itemsPrice`, `shippingPrice`, `taxPrice`, `totalPrice`.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Cart as a client-side Redux state structure with cart items and price totals, not persisted to database.

text:
### Checkout
Multi-step process where customers provide shipping address, payment method, and review before finalizing an order. Typically 2–4 steps depending on design (e.g., Address → Payment → Review → Confirm).

**In proshop:** Implemented as multi-screen flow in `frontend/src/screens/`. Backend validates order via `/api/orders` POST endpoint.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Checkout as the multi-step process from shipping address to order confirmation backed by POST /api/orders.

text:
### Review
User-submitted feedback on a product, containing a rating (1–5 stars), comment text, and author (customer). Reviews influence product's aggregate `rating` and `numReviews` count.

**In proshop:** Embedded in `Product.reviews[]` with fields: `rating`, `comment`, `user` (ObjectId ref), `name`, `createdAt`. Submitted via `POST /api/products/:id/reviews`.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Review as embedded product feedback with 1–5 star rating affecting aggregate product rating and review count.

text:
### Rating
Numeric score (0–5 stars) aggregating all reviews for a product. Calculated as mean of all individual review ratings. Updated when a review is added.

**In proshop:** Stored on product as `rating` (float) and `numReviews` (count). Both updated when new review is created.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Rating as the arithmetic mean of all product reviews, stored as a denormalized field updated on each new review.

text:
### Wishlist (Optional)
Personal list of products a customer wants to purchase later. Not fully implemented in base proshop but could be added as a feature.

**Related term:** Bookmark, Save for later.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Wishlist as an optional feature for customers to save products for later purchase, not yet implemented.

text:
## Technical Terms (Implementation Details)

### JWT (JSON Web Token)
Stateless authentication token containing encoded user info (ID, role, email). Sent in request headers for authorization. Server verifies token signature to ensure authenticity.

**In proshop:** Issued on login (`POST /api/users/login`). Token contains `userId` and `isAdmin`. Verified by `protect` middleware. Example header: `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc...`

**Security note:** Never expose `JWT_SECRET` in code. Rotate for production.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines JWT as the stateless auth token issued on login, verified by protect middleware, with security warning about JWT_SECRET.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines bcrypt as the password hashing library used in the User model pre-save hook with salt rounds of 10.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Mongoose as the ODM for MongoDB providing schema validation, hooks, and query methods used across all three models.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Express Middleware as request/response interceptors, illustrated with the protect JWT verification middleware.

text:
### REST Endpoint
HTTP endpoint following REST conventions: `GET /api/products` (list), `POST /api/products` (create), `PUT /api/products/:id` (update), `DELETE /api/products/:id` (delete).

**In proshop:** All routes follow REST:
- `GET /api/products` → list all products.
- `GET /api/products/:id` → get one product.
- `POST /api/orders` → create order.
- `PUT /api/orders/:id/pay` → mark order as paid.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines REST Endpoint conventions with examples of the product and order routes used in ProShop.

text:
### ObjectId
MongoDB's native unique identifier format (12-byte hex string like `"60d5ec49c1234567890abcde"`). Used as primary key for every document.

**In proshop:** Every document has `_id` field (ObjectId). Foreign keys use ObjectId refs. Example: `Order.user` = ObjectId of the customer.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines ObjectId as MongoDB's 12-byte primary key used for document IDs and foreign key references across collections.

text:
### Virtual Field
Mongoose computed field not stored in database. Calculated on-the-fly from other fields. Useful for derived data.

**In proshop:** Could add virtual like `product.totalReviewScore = reviews.length * avgRating` (not stored, computed when accessed).

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Virtual Field as a Mongoose computed property not stored in MongoDB, calculated on-the-fly from other fields.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Mongoose populate() method that replaces ObjectId references with full document data, used in order queries.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Async Handler as the express-async-handler wrapper that propagates async errors to Express error middleware.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Feature Flag as a runtime toggle for enabling/disabling code paths without redeployment, used for canary and A/B testing.

text:
### Canary Deployment
Gradual rollout of new code to subset of users (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) to catch bugs early without affecting all users.

**In proshop:** Implemented via feature flag's `rolloutPercentage`. Start at 5% for 1–2 hours, watch metrics, expand if stable.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Canary Deployment as gradual 5%→25%→50%→100% rollout implemented via feature flag's rolloutPercentage.

text:
### Rollback
Revert to previous code version (git revert or Heroku release rollback). Fastest way to stop a broken deploy from harming users.

**In proshop:** `heroku releases:rollback v20` reverts to release 20 in <1 minute. No code change needed.

**When to use:** Incident detected within 5 minutes of deploy.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Rollback as reverting to a previous release, with the Heroku releases:rollback command as the mechanism.

text:
### Blue-Green Deployment
Strategy where two identical production environments (blue=current, green=new) run in parallel. Switch traffic from blue to green once green is verified. Allows instant rollback.

**In proshop:** Not currently implemented (Heroku doesn't natively support blue-green). Could be added with load balancer + multiple dynos.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Blue-Green Deployment as parallel environment strategy for instant rollback, not yet implemented in ProShop.

text:
### Health Check
Simple endpoint or query to verify service is running and responsive. Used for monitoring and automated restarts.

**In proshop:** Could implement `GET /api/health` returning `{ status: "OK", uptime: 3600 }`. Monitored every 30 seconds.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Health Check as a GET /api/health endpoint used for monitoring service availability and automated restarts.

text:
### Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
Message queue for failed/unprocessable messages. Prevents infinite retries of bad data and preserves data for analysis.

**In proshop:** Not currently used. If emails fail, they could go to DLQ instead of being retried infinitely.

**Concept:** Order confirmation email fails → message moved to DLQ → manual review later.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Dead Letter Queue as a holding queue for failed messages preventing infinite retries, not currently used in ProShop.

text:
### SLA (Service Level Agreement)
Commitment to uptime/performance. Example: "99.9% uptime" = max 43 minutes downtime/month.

**In proshop:** Hypothetical SLA: 99% uptime, P95 latency < 1 second, checkout success rate > 99.5%.

### SLO (Service Level Objective)
Internal target stricter than SLA. Example: SLA is 99% uptime; SLO is 99.5% (buffer for incidents).

**In proshop:** SLO: 99.5% uptime, P99 latency < 2 seconds (more aggressive than SLA to catch issues early).

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines SLA as the committed uptime/performance target and SLO as the stricter internal objective with error budget.

text:
### P95 Latency
Response time where 95% of requests complete faster. Measures typical user experience (ignores slow outliers).

**In proshop:** "P95 latency is 800ms" = 95% of requests complete in <800ms, 5% take longer. If P95 suddenly spikes to 5000ms, incident likely.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines P95 Latency as the response time below which 95% of requests complete, used as a key performance monitoring metric.

text:
## Business & Metrics Terms

### AOV (Average Order Value)
Total revenue divided by number of orders. Indicator of purchase size and monetization.

**In proshop:** If 1000 orders totaled $45,000, AOV = $45. Goal: increase AOV through upsells, bundles, etc.

**Formula:** `AOV = Total Revenue / Total Orders`

### GMV (Gross Merchandise Value)
Total value of orders placed (before refunds, cancellations). Gross measure of platform activity.

**In proshop:** If 1000 orders placed with AOV $45, GMV = $45,000.

**Formula:** `GMV = Number of Orders × Average Order Value`

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines AOV (average revenue per order) and GMV (total order value before refunds) as core e-commerce business metrics.

text:
### Conversion Rate
Percentage of visitors who complete a purchase. Critical metric for e-commerce health.

**In proshop:** If 10,000 users visit, 300 place orders, conversion rate = 3%. Industry avg: 1–3%.

**Formula:** `Conversion Rate = Orders / Visitors × 100%`

### Cart Abandonment Rate
Percentage of shopping carts created but not converted to orders. Indicates friction in checkout.

**In proshop:** If 5000 carts created but only 1500 complete checkout, abandonment = 70%. High abandonment suggests checkout is too complex.

**Formula:** `Cart Abandonment = (Carts Created - Orders Completed) / Carts Created × 100%`

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Conversion Rate (orders/visitors) and Cart Abandonment Rate as key e-commerce funnel health metrics.

text:
### Repeat Purchase Rate
Percentage of customers who buy more than once. Indicator of customer satisfaction and retention.

**In proshop:** If 1000 customers exist and 200 placed 2+ orders, repeat rate = 20%. Goal: >30% indicates healthy business.

**Formula:** `Repeat Rate = Repeat Customers / Total Customers × 100%`

### NPS (Net Promoter Score)
Survey metric measuring customer loyalty. Customers rate "likelihood to recommend" 0–10. Detractors (0–6) subtract from Promoters (9–10).

**In proshop:** Could add post-checkout survey. Target: >50 is healthy.

**Formula:** `NPS = % Promoters - % Detractors`

### Churn Rate
Percentage of customers who stop using service in a period. Opposite of retention.

**In proshop:** If 1000 active customers last month, 50 never return, churn = 5%. High churn = problem with product/service.

**Formula:** `Churn = Lost Customers / Starting Customers × 100%`

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Repeat Purchase Rate, NPS, and Churn Rate as customer retention and loyalty metrics for e-commerce health.

text:
## Cross-Cutting Reference

### Environment Variables
Configuration passed at runtime (not hardcoded). Examples: `NODE_ENV`, `MONGO_URI`, `JWT_SECRET`, `PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID`.

**In proshop:** Stored in `.env` (local) or Heroku config vars (production). Always `.env` is gitignored for security.

### Node.js
JavaScript runtime for server-side code. Allows JavaScript to run outside browser. Event-driven, non-blocking I/O.

**In proshop:** Backend runs on Node.js v14+. `npm start` launches Node.js process running `backend/server.js`.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines environment variables and Node.js runtime, covering .env configuration and the backend server entry point.

text:
### npm (Node Package Manager)
JavaScript package manager. `npm install` pulls dependencies from registry. `package.json` lists all dependencies.

**In proshop:** `npm install` downloads 50+ packages (express, mongoose, bcryptjs, etc.).

### Docker
Containerization platform. `docker compose up` starts containers (MongoDB) without installing MongoDB locally.

**In proshop:** `npm run mongo:up` = `docker compose up -d mongo` = starts MongoDB in isolated container. Same image everywhere (dev, staging, prod).

### Heroku
Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) for hosting Node.js apps. Handles deployment, scaling, and infrastructure.

**In proshop:** `git push heroku main` deploys code. Heroku builds Docker image, runs `npm install`, executes `heroku-postbuild` script, starts dyno.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines npm, Docker, and Heroku as the package management, containerization, and deployment tools used in ProShop.

text:
### Idempotent
Operation that produces same result if run multiple times. Safe to retry.

**In proshop:** `npm run data:import` is idempotent—running twice is safe (seed script deletes then recreates data). Non-idempotent: `POST /api/orders` without check—running twice creates 2 orders.

### Atomicity
Database guarantee that operation completes fully or not at all. No partial updates.

**In proshop:** If order creation involves: 1) create order, 2) decrement inventory, 3) charge payment, all three must succeed or all rollback. MongoDB transactions ensure atomicity.

metadata.title: ProShop MERN E-Commerce Glossary
metadata.summary: Defines Idempotent (safe to retry) and Atomicity (all-or-nothing) as data reliability guarantees relevant to order processing.
